core_meaning: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> has two almost opposite meanings depending on context: (1) 'appropriate / suitable' (the original meaning, more common in formal/written Japanese), and (2) 'half-assed / sloppy / random' (the colloquial meaning, very common in daily speech). In casual conversation, it almost always means someone is doing something carelessly or without real effort.

sentences.jp: あの<ruby>店員<rt>てんいん</rt></ruby>、<ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な<ruby>返事<rt>へんじ</rt></ruby>しかしてくれなかった。
sentences.translation: That shop clerk gave me nothing but half-assed answers.
sentences.notes: This shows the very common colloquial sense — <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な＋noun meaning 'careless' or 'sloppy.'

sentences.jp: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>にやっといたら、<ruby>上司<rt>じょうし</rt></ruby>にめちゃくちゃ<ruby>怒<rt>おこ</rt></ruby>られた。
sentences.translation: I did it half-heartedly and my boss got super mad at me.
sentences.notes: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>にやる is a very natural collocation meaning 'to do something sloppily or without care.'

sentences.jp: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な<ruby>時間<rt>じかん</rt></ruby>に<ruby>来<rt>き</rt></ruby>てください。
sentences.translation: Please come at a suitable time.
sentences.notes: This is the formal/original meaning — 'appropriate' or 'suitable.' Context makes it clear this is not the negative sense.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>仕事<rt>しごと</rt></ruby>には<ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な<ruby>人材<rt>じんざい</rt></ruby>を<ruby>選<rt>えら</rt></ruby>ぶ<ruby>必要<rt>ひつよう</rt></ruby>があります。
sentences.translation: It is necessary to select personnel appropriate for this job.
sentences.notes: In formal or written contexts, <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> reliably means 'suitable' — the negative connotation is rare in this register.

sentences.jp: 「<ruby>晩<rt>ばん</rt></ruby>ごはん<ruby>何<rt>なに</rt></ruby>がいい？」「<ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>でいいよ。」
sentences.translation: 'What do you want for dinner?' 'Anything is fine / Whatever works.'
sentences.notes: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>でいい is a natural casual phrase meaning 'anything's fine' — relaxed rather than negative here, showing a softer shade of the word.

dont_use: Don't use <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> when you mean 'correct' or 'accurate' — it does not mean that. Also be careful using it in formal writing to mean 'suitable': readers may need context to disambiguate it from the sloppy nuance. Avoid saying <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>にやります to a boss or client — even if you mean 'I'll handle it appropriately,' it often sounds like 'I'll just wing it.'

confused_with.contrast: Learners sometimes use <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> where <ruby>適切<rt>てきせつ</rt></ruby> is clearly the right choice — for example, saying <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な<ruby>言葉<rt>ことば</rt></ruby>を<ruby>使<rt>つか</rt></ruby>ってください when giving a careful instruction. <ruby>適切<rt>てきせつ</rt></ruby> has no colloquial 'sloppy' connotation and always means 'appropriate/proper,' making it the safer word in formal or instructional contexts. Use <ruby>適切<rt>てきせつ</rt></ruby> when you want 'appropriate' with zero ambiguity.

confusion_set.use_when: Casual speech for 'sloppy/half-assed,' OR neutral 'suitable' in less formal writing — but know it's ambiguous.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>にやったら<ruby>失敗<rt>しっぱい</rt></ruby>した。

confusion_set.use_when: When you mean 'appropriate/proper' with no risk of the 'sloppy' reading — the safe formal choice.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>適切<rt>てきせつ</rt></ruby>な<ruby>対応<rt>たいおう</rt></ruby>をお<ruby>願<rt>ねが</rt></ruby>いします。

confusion_set.use_when: When you want to emphasize irresponsibility or being half-hearted — stronger and more accusatory than <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>いい加減<rt>いいかげん</rt></ruby>なことを<ruby>言<rt>い</rt></ruby>わないで。

frequency: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> is extremely common in daily casual speech, almost always in its 'sloppy/half-hearted' sense. The 'suitable' meaning appears in formal writing and set phrases but is less frequent in conversation. One of the most useful words to know for understanding natural spoken Japanese.
anki_hint: Remember: in casual speech <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> almost always means 'sloppy/half-assed' — if you want unambiguous 'appropriate,' use <ruby>適切<rt>てきせつ</rt></ruby> instead.
